question.text: Under Section 90 of the Income Tax Act, a taxpayer who is a resident of a country with which India has a DTAA can be taxed under:

answer.text: Only the provisions of the Income Tax Act, irrespective of DTAA terms
Only the provisions of the DTAA, irrespective of the Income Tax Act
Either the Income Tax Act or the DTAA, whichever is more beneficial to the taxpayer
The average of tax computed under both the Income Tax Act and the DTAA

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Section 90 allows the taxpayer the benefit of choosing between the provisions of the Income Tax Act and the DTAA — whichever results in a lower tax liability. This is a taxpayer-friendly provision ensuring the DTAA never increases the tax burden beyond what the domestic law imposes. **The Trap:** The DTAA does not override the IT Act, nor does the IT Act override the DTAA — the taxpayer gets the more beneficial treatment. "Average of both" is a fabricated concept with no legal basis. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series X-A covers the basics of Section 90. Level 2 requires deeper understanding of how the choice operates in practice. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always compute tax under both the IT Act and the DTAA for your client and present the lower figure — this is a statutory right, not a concession.
